coredata.dc:title: Nature-driven blue-emissive N, S-CDs: Harnessing sequential “switch-off-on” fluorescence signals for detection of chrysin and Al³⁺ along with cellular imaging versatility
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdel-Monem, Maha Mohammad
Walash, Mohamed I.
El-Deen, Asmaa Kamal
coredata.dc:description: Novel biocompatible blue emissive nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized via in situ microwave-assisted synthesis from snake melon (Cucumis melo var. flexuosus) for the first time. The N, S-CDs have been utilized for fluorimetric determination of chrysin (CHR). The fluorescence emitted by the N, S-CDs (λem = 460 nm) decreased upon the addition of CHR due to dynamic quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE). The reduction in emission intensity exhibits a linear relationship over a concentration range of 2.0–275.0 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0837 μM. By contrast, the quenched fluorescence of the N, S-CD/CHR system was restored upon the addition of Al³⁺, which efficiently displaced CHR from the surface of the N, S-CDs, prompting fluorescence "switch-on". The retrieved fluorescence intensity was proportional to Al3+ concentration over a range of 50.0–4000.0 μM, with a LOD of 2.96 μM. This sequential approach was efficiently utilized to determine CHR and Al3+ in real pharmaceutical, biological, and environmental samples with accurate and precise findings. Furthermore, due to their minimal cytotoxicity, the N, S-CDs were also successful for cellular imaging on HepG2 cells. The proposed sensing platform provides several merits, including simplicity, cost-efficiency, long-lasting stability, high selectivity, and exceptional sensitivity. These findings not only contribute to the growing field of carbon-based nanomaterials but also offer a sustainable and multifunctional platform for environmental and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chrysin
Al3+
Carbon dots
Microwave synthesis
Fluorescence
Cell imaging

The optical properties of N, S-CDs

Optimization of reaction variables for the determination of chrysin
Mechanisms of fluorimetric CHR determination (fluorescence quenching)
Validation of the developed sensing platforms for the determination of chrysin and Al3+
Determination of CHR in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the N, S-CDs, where (a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) zeta potential, (c) zeta sizer, (d) TEM image, (e) SEM image, and (f) EDX pattern.
(a) XPS full spectrum of N, S-CDs, (b–e) The high-resolution C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p spectra of the N, S-CDs.

(a) UV–Visible spectra along with excitation and emission of N, S-CDs (water as a blank) [inset: images of the CDs at visible light and under 365 nm UV-light], (b) excitation-independent emission of the N, S-CDs at λex range of 310–390 nm, (c–f) factors affecting the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs at λex/em of 375/460 nm [(c) pH of 0.04 M BRB, (d) %NaCl, (e) temperature, (f) photostability, and (g) storage at 4 °C].

A co-plot of the UV–Visible spectrum of CHR in ethanol and CHR at pH 8 and the excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CDs, (b) suppressed efficiency of the observed and corrected fluorescence measurements of N, S-CDs after adding different concentrations of CHR in the range of 2.0–275.0 μM, and (c) Stern-Volmer plots of CDs-CHR at various temperatures (303, 313, 323, and 333 K) at CHR concentrations from 20.0 to 236.0 μM.
A schematic diagram for the synthesis of N, S-CDs.
Analytical Performance data for the determination of CHR and Al 3+by the developed fluorimetric methods.
Precision and accuracy data for the determination of CHR and Al 3+by the developed fluorimetric methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three replicates.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values between parentheses are the tabulated t- and F-values at P = 0.05.

entry.bold: Hemostatic Liquid® dental solution (25.0 % Aluminium Chloride)

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each reading is the average of three replicates.

head.ce:title: Nature-driven blue-emissive N, S-CDs: Harnessing sequential “switch-off-on” fluorescence signals for detection of chrysin and Al³⁺ along with cellular imaging versatility

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aPharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Agro-Environmental Sciences, Faculty of Agriculture, Kyushu University, 819-0395, Fukuoka, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Agro-Environmental Sciences, Faculty of Agriculture, Kyushu University, 819-0395, Fukuoka, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel biocompatible blue emissive nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were synthesized via in situ microwave-assisted synthesis from snake melon (Cucumis melo var. flexuosus) for the first time. The N, S-CDs have been utilized for fluorimetric determination of chrysin (CHR). The fluorescence emitted by the N, S-CDs (λem = 460 nm) decreased upon the addition of CHR due to dynamic quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE). The reduction in emission intensity exhibits a linear relationship over a concentration range of 2.0–275.0 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0837 μM. By contrast, the quenched fluorescence of the N, S-CD/CHR system was restored upon the addition of Al³⁺, which efficiently displaced CHR from the surface of the N, S-CDs, prompting fluorescence "switch-on". The retrieved fluorescence intensity was proportional to Al3+ concentration over a range of 50.0–4000.0 μM, with a LOD of 2.96 μM. This sequential approach was efficiently utilized to determine CHR and Al3+ in real pharmaceutical, biological, and environmental samples with accurate and precise findings. Furthermore, due to their minimal cytotoxicity, the N, S-CDs were also successful for cellular imaging on HepG2 cells. The proposed sensing platform provides several merits, including simplicity, cost-efficiency, long-lasting stability, high selectivity, and exceptional sensitivity. These findings not only contribute to the growing field of carbon-based nanomaterials but also offer a sustainable and multifunctional platform for environmental and biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chrysin (CHR) is a natural flavonoid (Table S1) found in several plant sources that possesses numerous health benefits [1,2]. It has been employed as a component in medications and nutritional supplements owing to its ability to inhibit several enzymes responsible for generating superoxide (O2 −) and peroxyl radicals, as well as to scavenge these radicals due to the presence of the 3‑hydroxyl group and ring B's catechol group which promotes its antioxidant activity [3]. Therefore, establishing sensitive and reliable analytical methods for accurate CHR detection is crucial. There are numerous analytical techniques for the assay of CHR, including spectrophotometry [4–7], spectrofluorimetry [8], electrochemistry [9–11], HPLC [12–17], UPLC [18–21], and capillary electrophoresis [22]. Traditional techniques for CHR assay often necessitate expensive, sophisticated equipment and skilled personnel. Furthermore, they may lack sufficient sensitivity or necessitate the use of hazardous chemicals or organic solvents. Consequently, there is a pressing need to develop sensitive, cost-effective, and user-friendly sensing platforms for the determination of CHR. To our knowledge, there is just one fluorimetric approach for CHR estimation [8]].

Aluminum (Al3+), another critical analyte, is considered an important analyte in pharmaceutical and environmental samples. It is considered the most common ingredient in antiperspirants, and it has been recently utilized as a hemostatic agent in dentistry [23]. It is commonly found in trace amounts in water, soil, food, and biological systems. Although healthy people can eliminate virtually all ingested Al3+, excessive amounts of Al3+ can cause oxidative stress in the liver, kidneys, and brain. Exposure to Al3+ can alter or inhibit a variety of enzymes, affecting protein synthesis and permeability of cell membranes [24]. This might have a variety of deleterious consequences, including decreased glomerular filtration, changes in blood levels of creatinine, urea, and uric acid [25]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has established a Supplementary Maximum Contaminant Level (SMCL) of 0.05–0.2 ppm for aluminum in drinking water [26]. Therefore, its determination is crucial for monitoring environmental pollution, assessing water quality, and studying the bioaccumulation of metals in living organisms. This prompted us to a new sequential sensing platform for the fluorescence-based analysis of CHR and Al3+in various matrices.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have lately gained popularity as photoluminescent materials with potential chemical, electrical, and optical characteristics [27]. They have been investigated for cellular bioimaging, biomarker detection, and pharmaceutical sensing owing to their low toxicity, great chemical stability, and high water solubility [28]. Recently, CDs have been considered an excellent alternative for fluorescent derivatizing agents and fluorescent dyes [29]. The intrinsic photoluminescence of CDs allows for establishing a sensing platform for fluorimetric detection methods, resulting in improved detection limits and enhanced sensitivity and selectivity [30,31]. Several ways have been established for preparing CDs, like chemical oxidation, electrochemical, hydrothermal, and microwave-assisted methods, either from chemical or natural sources [28,29]. Microwave-assisted synthesis is the fastest, easiest, least expensive, and most environmentally friendly [32–35]. Recently, the sustainable synthesis of CDs from renewable sources and recyclable materials has garnered great attention [36–38].

In this work, we synthesized N, S-CDs using one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis (4 min) using snake melon fruit (Cucumis melo L.) extract, a novel renewable precursor rich in polyphenols, carotenoids, unsaturated fatty acids, folates, triterpenoids, and vitamin C [39–41]. The resulting N, S-CDs exhibited high water solubility, excellent stability, and intense blue emission, serving as an efficient fluorimetric probe. Initially, the system was utilized to detect CHR through fluorescence quenching (via IFE and dynamic quenching) and was efficiently applied to real samples (capsules, human plasma, and environmental water). Remarkably, subsequent addition of Al³⁺ restored the fluorescence, enabling a sequential "switch-off-on" sensing platform for Al³⁺ determination. This dual-function probe offers high sensitivity, selectivity, and applicability across pharmaceutical, environmental, and clinical investigations.
Chemicals, instruments, and details of the experimental procedures for stock preparations and theoretical calculations can be found in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh snake melons were brought from the Egyptian local market and cleaned completely with tap water, then distilled water, and cut into little pieces. After that, 250.0 g was transferred to a mixed home mixer with 50.0 mL of distilled water, followed by filtration of the obtained juice with gauze. Consequently, 50.0 mL of that juice was moved into a beaker and then placed in a household microwave oven for 4 min to obtain a charred residue (Scheme 1 ). The residue was allowed to cool down to room temperature before being mixed with 50.0 mL of distilled water, sonicated, and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min. A 0.45 μm syringe filter was utilized to filter the obtained supernatant. The resulting dark-brown solution remained stable for over three months when kept in an amber-colored bottle at 4 °C in the refrigerator for later use. The N, S-CDs were further characterized using different techniques, including FTIR, Zeta-potential, TEM, XPS, EDX, fluorescence, and UV–Vis spectra.

CHR solutions with concentrations ranging from 2.0 to 275.0 μM were placed into 10.0 mL volumetric flasks, followed by the addition of 1.0 mL BRB at pH 8. After that, 120.0 μL of N, S-CDs had been added, followed by distilled water, and well mixed. A calibration curve between the concentration of CHR in μM and the quenched fluorescence (F0-F) was created.

The Al3+ determination process involved adding 1.75 mL of CHR (1573.0 μM), 1.0 mL of pH 8 BRB, and 120.0 μL of N, S-CDs to a series of 10.0 mL volumetric flasks. Then, several amounts of Al3+ covering the linear range (50.0–4000.0 μM) were added, and the flask was filled to the mark with distilled water and mixed thoroughly. A calibration plot of relative fluorescence intensity (FI, enhanced fluorescence) (F-F0) versus Al3+concentration was created. For all studies, the FI was measured at 460 nm following excitation at 375 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for chrysin determination in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Spiked human plasma samples were prepared by protein precipitation to extract the desired analyte from plasma components and minimize interference from plasma components as follows: In a series of 15.0 mL centrifuge tubes, 1.0 mL of methanol was added to 0.50 mL of human plasma previously spiked with CHR (methanolic solution). After that, the tubes were vortexed for 2 min and then centrifuged for 20 min at 5000 rpm. Then, the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.45 μm filter syringe. Finally, 0.5 mL BRB of pH 8 and 60.0 μL of N, S-CDs were added, and the final volume was completed to 5.0 mL with water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for chrysin determination in capsules
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of ten capsules were thoroughly combined and precisely weighed. Then, an accurately weighed amount of the powder equivalent to 20.0 mg of CHR was transferred into a 100.0 mL volumetric flask, and 50.0 mL of methanol was added. After 30 min of sonication, the contents of the flask were completed to the mark using the same solvent. The solution was filtered into another 100.0 mL volumetric flask. Working solutions were produced by further dilution with water for analysis by the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for chrysin and Al3+ determination in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: River water samples (collected from Nile River, Mansoura, Egypt) and tap water samples (collected from our laboratory, Mansoura, Egypt) were analyzed by the proposed method after sample pretreatment procedures such as centrifugation and filtration to remove any possible contaminants and eliminate interferences that may arise from the presence of various organic and inorganic compounds. The collected samples were found to be free from the studied analytes, or they might be present in a concentration lower than the detection limit of the developed method. Therefore, they were directly analyzed following the proposed method. After being spiked, the percent recovery was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for dental solution for Al3+ determination
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of 10.0 mM of Al3+ from dental solution was prepared in distilled water. By further diluting this solution with pure water, various concentrations were obtained.
Details for the procedures for both cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging are explained in the Supplementary material.

Snake melon is extremely rich in organic molecules such as protein, carbohydrates, and amino acids, including cysteine (a source of sulfur) and other nitrogen-containing compounds. The chemical components in snake melon undergo carbonization and break down into smaller carbon pieces when exposed to microwave radiation. Nitrogen and sulfur atoms from amino acids and other organic substances in snake melon are absorbed into the carbon matrix, forming the N, S-CDs. The tiny size of CDs causes quantum confinement, which results in distinctive levels of energy and photoluminescence. Functional groups (such as -OH, -COOH, -SH, and -NH2) on the surface of N, S-CDs provide surface states that can trap electrons and holes, resulting in photoluminescence and radiative recombination. Furthermore, the integration of nitrogen and sulfur introduces extra levels of energy within the bandgap, which improves photoluminescence characteristics.

Several techniques were used to characterize the CDs. The surface functional groups of N, S-CDs were examined by FTIR analysis, as shown in Fig. 1a . Stretching vibrations are shown by the wide range at 3377 cm−1 and the sharp peak at 1665 cm−1. The bands at 2853, 1588, 1260, 866, and 677 cm−1 represent CH, CC, CN, CH2, and C-S groups, respectively [42]. The presence of amine and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N, S-CDs resulted in a negative zeta potential value (− 18.36 mV, Fig. 1b). This also reveals the extraordinary stability of N, S-CDs. The hydrodynamic size of the N, S-CDs was mainly found to be 55.62 nm, as shown in Fig. 1c. The TEM and SEM pictures verify the synthesis of N, S-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1d and Fig. 1e; the as-synthesized N, S-CDs were distributed evenly as spherical nanoparticles with no apparent aggregation and a tiny particle size dispersion. Furthermore, the EDX pattern of the synthesized N, S-CDs is shown in Fig. 1f. The EDX results show that the samples are made up of C, N, O, and S elements. Furthermore, it demonstrates that N, S-CDs have the necessary composition without any additional impurities. Additionally, XPS spectra have been collected to better analyze the surface groups of the as-synthesized CDs. The XPS complete survey spectrum of the N, S-CDs, given in Fig. 2a , shows the characteristic peaks of C1s (286.42 eV), N1s (401.12 eV), O1s (533.30 eV), and S2p3 (171.08 eV). Fig. 2b depicts three signals in the C1s spectrum: 285.04 eV (CC), 286.46 eV (CC), and 288.57 eV (CN/CO). The O1s spectra have three peaks at 531.65 eV (CO), 532.95 eV (OH), and 533.65 eV (CO/COC), as seen in Fig. 2c. The N1s spectra displayed in Fig. 2d demonstrate the existence of CN (399.74 eV) and pyrrolic nitrogen (401.10 eV). The S2p spectra in Fig. 2e display a doublet peak at 169.21 and 169.47 eV, which corresponds to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of C-SOx, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–VIS absorbance spectrum, as well as excitation and emission spectra, were utilized to assess the optical characteristics of the N, S-CDs. The absorbance peak observed at 230 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of NN/CC as can be noticed in Fig. 3a . Furthermore, the n–π∗ transition of C–NH/ CO located on the surface of N, S-CDs results in a secondary peak at approximately 300 nm, enabling emission at longer wavelengths. Moreover, the highest fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs, occurring at 460 nm with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm in Fig. 3a, exhibits a Stokes shift of 85 nm, surpassing values reported in the existing literature. A larger Stokes shift in the fluorescence platform can prevent reabsorption and enhance the emission efficiency of the fluorophore, presenting a significant benefit in chemical detection. As depicted in Fig. 3b, as the excitation wavelength increased from 310 nm to 390 nm, the emission wavelength of N, S-CDs remained relatively constant, but the FI notably rose. This pattern suggests that the N, S-CDs demonstrate characteristic excitation-dependent emission behavior resulting from diverse surface defects and functional groups. Moreover, some studies have linked the small redshift in fluorescence emission to the quantum size effects of the N, S-CDs. Further validation through XPS and FTIR analysis revealed the existence of varied surface functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH, C–S,…..) in the N, S-CDs, contributing to distinct surface state energies and emission traps, consequently leading to a progressive red-shift in emission wavelength with varying excitations. Additional research has suggested that the incorporation of–COOH and CN groups could diminish the energy level between the HOMO and LUMO bandgap, resulting in a small redshift in emission wavelength. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of N, S-CDs was computed to be 17.33 % utilizing the equation in Section S4, Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the N, S-CDs across different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of different environmental factors on the stability of the synthesized N, S-CDs has been investigated, including pH, temperature, salt concentrations, and storage times, along with their photostability. First, the influence of pH was explored across a wide range from pH 3 to 12. The FI of N, S-CDs stayed constant until reaching a pH 8, after which it notably declined (Fig. 3c). This shift might be related to nitrogen atoms on the surface of N, S-CDs. In acidic solutions containing hydrogen ions, the lone pairs of electrons on these nitrogen atoms might form coordination bonds. Therefore, we suggest that low fluorescence intensity (FI) at higher pH levels is caused by the interaction of hydrogen and nitrogen atoms (pyrrole-like N, amino N, and pyridine-like N) as well as changes in the structure, dispersion, and homogeneity of the N, S-CDs. This hypothesis explains the rapid decrease in FI after pH 8. Nonetheless, the ability to sustain continuous fluorescence throughout a large pH range (pH 3 to 8.5) increases the versatility of our assay [43]. Moreover, the N, S-CDs exhibit exceptional stability even in environments with elevated salt concentrations. As depicted in Fig. 3d, the FI of the N, S-CDs remained relatively unchanged even at elevated NaCl concentrations (1.0 M). This characteristic is particularly valuable for the practical use of N, S-CDs in saline conditions. After that, the stability of N, S-CDs at different temperatures was studied. The FI was almost stable with minor changes even after changing the temperature from 25 to 50 °C (Fig. 3e). Additionally, we subjected the N, S-CDs to 60-minute continuous irradiation at 365 nm to evaluate their photostability, and the fluorescence remained generally unaffected (Fig. 3f), indicating stability over this duration. Ultimately, we tested the photobleaching capacity of the N, S-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3g, where the FI remained constant for more than three months when stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of reaction variables for the determination of chrysin
ce:section.ce:para: Various experimental factors that may affect the determination of CHR, including pH, buffer volume, CDs’ volume, and incubation time, were studied and optimized to perform the measurements under the optimum conditions and achieve the highest sensitivity. An OVAT (one-variable-at-a-time) optimization approach was utilized. Firstly, using the Britton Robinson buffer (BRB), the influence of pH was examined over a range of 3–12. At acidic pH ranges of 3–5, the efficacy of quenching fluorescence was low. After that, upon increasing pH, the fluorescence quenching efficiency improved till it reached a steady state. This may be attributed to the phenolic nature of CHR as the presence of an alkaline solution makes CHR undergo spectral shift and hyperchromic effect, increasing the overlap with the fluorescence spectra of the N, S-CDs and increasing the possibility of IFE. Therefore, pH 8 was utilized for further analysis (Fig. S1a). Moreover, the effect of different buffer volumes over a range of 0.50–5.0 mL was investigated. It was noticed that the buffer volume has no significant impact on the fluorescence quenching. Therefore, 1.0 mL has been selected for further analysis (Fig. S1b). Furthermore, the influence of N, S-CDs’ volume on the efficiency of fluorescence quenching was studied by varying its volume from 50.0 to 150.0 μL. The quenching effect was increased up to 100.0 μL; after that, a plateau was reached. Thus, 120.0 μL was selected as the optimum volume (Fig. S1c). Finally, the effect of incubation time on the quenching efficiency was studied. It was found that time does not affect fluorescence quenching (Fig. S1d). Therefore, the measurements were carried out instantaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of fluorimetric CHR determination (fluorescence quenching)
ce:section.ce:para:
Several potential quenching mechanisms, such as dynamic or static quenching and the IFE, can exist. Dynamic quenching is a nonradiative loss of energy caused by the collision between the fluorophore and the quencher, while static quenching arises from the interaction between the quencher and the fluorescent matter, leading to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex that immediately returns to the ground state without emission of light. The ground-state complex's formation may cause the CDs' absorption spectra to change. Elevating the temperature in the case of dynamic quenching improves molecular collision and increases the value of the Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv), while in the case of static quenching, the generated ground state complex cleaves as the temperature rises, leading to a reduction in the Ksv value [44,45]. Dynamic quenching is viscosity-dependent, while static quenching is temperature-dependent. The IFE takes place when the excitation spectra of the fluorophore and the quencher's absorption spectrum overlap, but it is not due to molecular interactions. It arises from optical artifacts caused by the presence of absorbing species in the sample. The absorption peaks of the CDs would remain unchanged since the IFE process does not belong to the static or dynamic quenching process [44,46]. On the other hand, the IFE is independent of temperature, and it does not display features of static or dynamic quenching. To study the mechanism behind CHR's quenching behavior, the excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CDs, as well as the absorbance band of CHR at pH 8, were recorded and integrated into a single graph to determine the degree of overlap. As illustrated in (Fig. 4a ), the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CHR overlaps with the excitation spectrum of N, S-CDs, demonstrating that IFE plays a significant role in the quenching mechanism. For further information about the potential contribution of the IFE, corrected fluorescence was calculated as follows:
(1)
F
C o r r e c t e d
=
F
O b s e r v e d
× A n t i l o g
(
A
e x
+
A
e m
2
)
Where Fobserved represents the observed FI while FCorrected indicates the FI after excluding the IFE, Aex, and Aem, referring to the absorbance of CHR at the wavelength of excitation and emission of the N, S-CDs, respectively. After that, considering the corrected and observed FI, the percentage suppressed efficiency (% E) was calculated according to the following equation:
(2)
%
E =
[
1 −
(
F /
F
0
)
]
× 100

The relationship between % E and CHR concentration (μM), shown in Fig. 4b, demonstrates that IFE has a significant impact on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. This could be observed in the 42.2 % reduction in suppression efficiency that occurs when IFE is deducted. Nevertheless, 57.8 % of the fluorescence quenching is still caused by another mechanism. Consequently, the Stern-Volmer equation has been used in the following manner to comprehend the possible quenching mechanism:
(3)
F /
F
0
= 1 +
K
s v
[
C H R
]
Where [CHR] is the micromolar concentration of chrysin, F0 and F are the FIs before and after CHR addition, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, it is calculated from the slope of (F0/F) versus [CHR]. Consequently, Stern-Volmer plots were obtained by plotting (F0/F) versus CHR concentration at four different temperatures (303, 313, 323, 333 K). As noticed in (Fig. 4c) and Table S2, the Ksv values increase upon increasing the temperature, suggesting a dynamic quenching mechanism. The molecular collision is enhanced upon increasing the temperature, leading to higher dynamic quenching at elevated temperatures. It has been found that CHR interacts with the excited state of N, S-CDs via collisions, resulting in non-radiative deactivation of the excited state. In detail, CHR molecules diffuse across the solution and collide with excited N, S-CDs. During the collision, energy or electrons are transmitted from the excited N, S-CDs to the CHR, leading to non-radiative decay of the excited state. Furthermore, dynamic quenching limits the fluorescence lifespan of N, S-CDs by deactivating the excited state before it can release a photon. This integrated process provides a sensitive and consistent way for determining CHR with N, S-CDs as a fluorescent probe. The experimental confirmation of both IFE and dynamic quenching provides precise quantification of CHR in a variety of applications.

The resulting CHR/ N, S-CDs system has been utilized for the determination of Al3+. The addition of Al3+was found to recover the fluorescence of N, S-CDs. The recovery effect of Al3+on the CHR/N, S-CDs system may be attributed to the capacity of Al3+ to strongly chelate with CHR, forming the CHR/Al3+complex [47,48]. The fluorescence recovery (enhancement) was found to be quantitative with Al3+concentrations. Consequently, this phenomenon could be utilized for Al3+estimation in various matrices. Fig. S2 represents the proposed mechanism for the CHR reaction with Al3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the developed sensing platforms for the determination of chrysin and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed approach was validated according to ICH guidelines [49] to ensure its reliability and precision for CHR and Al3+ estimation. Different validation parameters were studied as follows:

For CHR
: The calibration graph for CHR was generated by plotting (F0-F) (Fig. S3a) against CHR concentration in μM. The linear range of the method, which is the concentration range over which the procedure yields reliable measurements, was determined. Under optimum conditions, the proposed method possessed good linearity (R2 > 0.999) over a concentration range of 2.0–275.0 μM.

For Al3+
: The calibration graph for Al3+ was generated by plotting (F-F0) (Fig. S3b) against Al3+ concentration in μM. Under optimum conditions, the proposed method possessed good linearity (R2 > 0.999) for Al3+ over a concentration range of 50.0- 4000.0 μM.

According to ICH recommendations, both LOQ and LOD were calculated as in our previous reports from the standard deviation of its intercept [50,51]. The low values of LOQ and LOD for both CHR and Al3+ (Table 1 ) confirm the high sensitivity of our method for both analytes, indicating the method’s capacity for sequential estimation of the two analytes in various matrices.

Recovery experiments were performed to ensure the accuracy of the proposed approach. The method was applied for the determination of CHR and Al3+ in various real samples. The high percentage recoveries and low %error demonstrate the accuracy of our method. On the other hand, inter- and intra-day precision measurements were performed. For intra-day precision, different concentrations of CHR and Al3+were analyzed several times on the same day, while the measurements were carried out on three subsequent days to assess the inter-day precision. The developed method's precision was proven by its low %RSD values (< 2.0 %) as presented in Table 2 .

The selectivity of that developed probe for CHR was assessed. Several frequently encountered materials and components were investigated to ascertain their potential impact on the determination of CHR. A comprehensive analysis was conducted on a diverse range of drugs commonly co-administered, including rivaroxaban, paracetamol, albendazole, spiramycin, and mirtazapine. Furthermore, pharmaceutical additives or interference from plasm, including citric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose, lactose, starch, Na+, K +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, and HCO3 −, were also investigated. The results presented in Fig. S4a demonstrate minimal interference from commonly encountered interfering chemicals, indicating the exceptional selectivity of the fluorescent probe for CHR. Further studies were conducted to examine the selectivity of the CHR/N, S-CDs system. The system's fluctuation in FI (F-F0) was evaluated in the presence of several metal ions that may interfere with Al3+ detection, such as NH4 +, Na+, K +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, and Fe3+. The fluorescence of CHR/N, S-CDs was significantly restored after adding Al3+, while there was no obvious alteration for the other interfering substances (Fig. S4b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CHR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicality of the proposed method, we analyzed CHR in commercially available capsules, human plasma, and environmental water samples (river and tap water) following the experimental procedure. Table 3 demonstrates that the acquired results were excellent, with a recovery ranging from 97.8 to 101.5 % and small SD values (< 2.0). For further validation, we compared the developed method with a reported spectrophotometric approach [7] using the student t-test and the variance ratio F-test. As shown in Table 3, no significant differences were observed between the two methods, indicating that the proposed fluorometric sensor performs comparably to the established spectrophotometric technique while offering potential advantages in sensitivity, cost, or simplicity.

The developed fluorescent “switch-on” sensing platform has been applied for Al3+ estimation in river water, tap water, and dental solutions. The low % RSD (<2.0 %) and high % recoveries (>97.9 %) (Table 4 ) demonstrate the method’s applicability.

Cancer cells were co-cultured with CD doses ranging from 0.01 to 100.0 μg/mL for four hours. The MTT assay (Fig. S5a and Fig. S5b) showed that the average cell viability was above 72.0 % for the N, S-CD doses up to 1.0 μg/mL and fell to 60.0 % at 100.0 μg/mL. This finding implies that the N, S-CDs were relatively non-toxic to HepG2 cells, making them potentially useful for cell imaging or other medicinal applications. Moreover, cells treated with the CD's IC50 dose (3.18 μg/mL) showed brilliant fluorescence intensity (+++) when excited at 480 nm, indicating substantial cellular uptake while sustaining cell viability. This makes the proposed N, S-CDs a promising cellular imaging agent (Fig. S5c).

The proposed method was compared to previously described methods for CHR determination (Table S3). For instance, a facile and precise RP-HPLC technique for evaluating four bioactive phytochemicals (CHR is one of them) from a root bark was developed. The LOD was 166.78 μM. Furthermore, only one fluorimetric method was reported for CHR determination based on quenching the fluorescence of copper nanoclusters. Our fluorimetric approach is more sensitive with a wider concentration range than most of the reported methods. It offers a detection range similar to, but lower than, previously described electrochemical methods. Furthermore, it provides a significant advantage in terms of sensor cost, environmental friendliness, ease of preparation, and favorable environmental effects.

A novel sensing platform was developed for sensitive and selective determination of CHR in various matrices by quenching the fluorescence of N, S-CDs produced from snake melon (Cucumis melo var. flexuosus). These N, S-CDs have been synthesized via a microwave-assisted synthesis process in four minutes, eliminating the necessity for chemicals or solvents. This approach confers upon our work the advantages of simplicity, affordability, rapidity, and eco-friendliness. The as-synthesized N, S-CDs exhibit a high fluorescence quantum yield of 17.33 % along with water solubility and excellent stability. The study optimizes various parameters, establishing linear relationships and a low LOD of 0.0837 µM for CHR determination. Furthermore, the versatility of the synthesized sensor extends to the efficient “switch-on” detection of Al³⁺ ions, underscoring the promising role of N, S-CDs in biomedical and analytical fields. Due to its reliance on inexpensive, abundant, renewable, and non-toxic plant biomass, this approach eliminated the use of hazardous organic solvents or poisonous substances, while reducing the time, energy, and effort required. Consequently, the developed analytical method has placed a strong emphasis on sustainability. The developed approaches were successfully applied for the assay of CHR and Al³⁺ in real samples, including pharmaceutical formulations, human plasma, and environmental water samples. The high percentage recoveries achieved indicate the suitability of the developed sensors for clinical and environmental applications.

Maha Mohammad Abdel-Monem: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohamed I. Walash: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization. Asmaa Kamal El-Deen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.talo.2025.100466.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validation of analytical procedures: text and methodology
